Toxic language can be used in everyday conversation without even realizing it. It can start small, with insults or name-calling, but it can quickly escalate into more harmful behavior if left unchecked. For example, calling someone "stupid" or saying that they "have no idea what they're talking about" may seem harmless at first, but it can lead to hurt feelings and damaged relationships over time. Being a little bitch about does not help either, you should man up and shout right back at them. It's important to be mindful of our language and the impact it can have on others.

SANTA FE NEW HEX AGRICULTURAL COLLEGE Washington, May 10. W.O. In charge of the work at experiment stations, examined by the agricultural department, is preparing a bulletin which will be published this year, giving a sketch of the progress of education in agricultural colleges and schools. It is also noted that the purpose for which agricultural colleges were established in several states, and to which the government contributed by liberal grants of land and money, has not been realized. The colleges do not educate men for professions, and the tendency of their teachings has been to draw young men from farms instead of fitting them for work on them. The curriculum in most cases is too expensive for the average farmer\'s son to meet. The consequence is that the class for whom the colleges were assigned has received almost absolutely no benefit from their existence. In connection with the state university of Minnesota, the agricultural school was opened last fall, the course of study comprising two years of twenty-four weeks each. All graduates of the common district schools of the state qualified to enter, and they are taught practical things of farm life, in addition to the literary branches of learning. The school had been opened but a few weeks when its accommodations were all taken, while the agricultural college has been struggling along for an existence for years, with barely enough students to form a single class. At the Indian and colored schools at Hampton, Va., supported by the government, even a more thorough course of agricultural instruction is pursued, and nearly 100 pupils are engaged in it. The experiment stations of the department are located at all these places, and because of the great practical benefit accruing the bulletins will strongly advocate an extension of the system. Sec. Rusk is also deeply interested in the establishment of short winter courses of lectures to young men on farms, on topics relating to the successful conduct of their labors. A., T. & S. F. Business. Boston, May 10. The annual report of the directors of the A., T. & S. F. railroad for the year 1888 was given out yesterday. The gross earnings were $15,612,812, a decrease of $2,648,400. Operating expenses, including taxes, $11,027,151, an increase of $8,018,707. Net earnings, deducting taxes, $14,585,051, a doorcase of $3,467,100. Of the decrease of gross earnings, the loss on passenger business is $801,000, and on freight traffic $2,054,300. The deficiency for twelve months to December 31, was $1,517,880. Surplus December 31, $7,888,120. Net surplus, $0,435,551. The report says that even with the additional tariff derived by the Atchison system from the extensions, the actual number of passengers and tons of freight transported was considerably less than during the preceding year, and it is obvious that except from the business coming to it from its new lines, the earnings must have shown a still greater decrease. The reduction of business is due to various causes. Bold Bank Robbery. St. Joseph, May 8. At 3 o\'clock yesterday afternoon, Frazer & McDonald\'s bank, at Forest City, a small town twenty miles north of here, was entered by disguised men and robbed of $4,500. The robbery was committed by three men wearing long overcoats closely buttoned, slouch hats pulled down over their heads, and masks over their faces. They were not noticed till they walked into the front door of the building, where they met the president of the bank, Mr. C. K. Weber, talking to a farmer. They were both commanded to keep still, then they were covered by revolvers in the hands of two of the robbers. The other one having at about the same time covered the cashier, walked around behind the counter and producing a sack, he forced the cashier to open the safe and pour the contents, amounting to between $4,500 and $5,000, into it. The robbers escaped. A Lunatic\'s Wonderful Voice. Paris, May 10. An extraordinary discovery has just been made in the private asylum of Dr. Dupuis. A young Russian lady who entered the institution about eighteen months ago, and whose Malady is stated to be caused by a disappointment in love, is discovered to be possessed of a voice of the most marvelous beauty. The unfortunate girl\'s brain is in such a state that she cannot understand a word that is said to her, but as soon as music is placed in her hands her senses seem to return, and she renders the most difficult passages with the greatest intelligence and delicacy. If Dr. Dupuis can obtain the consent of the young lady\'s friends he intends to bring her out at a concert which is to begin shortly in this city in aid of a charity. A Street Car Smashed. Kalamazoo, Mich., May 9. A switch engine going at high speed dashed into a street car containing seven ladies and two men, as the latter was crossing the tracks at the west Main street crossing of the Michigan Central road. The driver was unable to get out of the way, and the passengers were hurled to a terrible death. Those in the car were Mrs. A. Haddock, Mrs. M. E. Watson, Gertrude Tillotson, Mrs. George Smiley, Mrs. Middleton, Mrs. Van Anthwerp, Mrs. S. A. Gibson, Mrs. Jonathan Barnes, Mr. J. C. Lull and Dr. Button. The first six were killed, two being found dead in a cattle yard, one in a creek near by, and one on the track, and Mrs. Middleton died an hour later. Veterans to Meet, New York. May 10. The Army of the Potomac will meet at Orange, N.J., on June 12 and 13. The society and corps meeting and banquet will be held on the first day and an excursion to West Point will be made on the following day. Hon. Cortland Parker will be the orator and Will Carleton the poet. The president, secretary of war, Gen. Sherman, Gen. Schofield and other distinguished officers are expected to be present. Kealh Dealing Winds in Kansas and the Northwest. HISTORICAL, Kas., May 0. The wind storm which has been raging for the last three days culminated in a cyclone in the northern part of Pratt county, extending over the eastern portion of Stafford and up into Rice county. The district over which it passed is sparsely settled, and but for this fact the loss of life and property would have been terrible. The cyclone first struck a spot five miles southeast of Station. In a northeasterly direction, left nothing but a barren waste strewn with wreckage of houses and barns, trees, and fences, and here and there with the bodies of dead and crippled stock. The only person killed outright was William Crawford, whose neck was broken by the flying timbers of his house, which was totally demolished. Nine other people were fatally injured, and about twenty or thirty others more or less injured. More than a hundred people are left homeless, without food or clothing. Sterling, Kas. As a sequel to three days of blustery weather, a fearful storm of wind, rain and hail, with thunder and lightning accompaniment, visited this section last night. The storm gathered south of the city and traveled north and west about four miles, thence south and east two and a half miles this side of Lyons, carrying death and destruction in its path. Twelve or fourteen barns, five houses, one church, one school house and numberless outbuildings were totally destroyed, the debris being scattered over the prairie for miles. The damage is estimated at $8,000. Hail did not injure crops materially, as far as heard from. Worse and Mote of It. St. Paul. The storm which has been gathering force since last Saturday reached its culmination yesterday in a gale blowing from twenty to thirty miles per hour. A similar gale was reported from all over Dakota, Minnesota and Manitoba, and in some cases considered an ague is feared. At Buffalo in this county the storm reached its greatest severity. Wheat in many places was blown out of the ground by the roots. In other places it is buried so deep it will never come up. At Muine, Prairie, Melrose, Hockville, Hawk Center and all the small towns in Stearn county, the wind unroofed dwellings and moved outbuildings from their foundations. A Fair Barbarian. Kansas. Crrv, May 10. Clara Graham, a beautiful girl of 18 years, has been convicted in the criminal court of horse stealing. She confessed that on February 13th, while out of work, she hired a horse and buggy and started out for a drive. While out, she met Frank McCoy on horseback and he joined her and proposed an elopement, she to go to St. Joseph. She went there and when he did not come, drove to Horton, Kas., and being out of money, sold the horse and buggy. The jury found her guilty and gave her two years. Destructive Conflagration. Chicago, May 10. Seventy families were rendered homeless by a fire which swept the suburban village of Moreland yesterday. Washington, May 11. Secretary Noble yesterday rendered an important decision in the case of James Hair, of Kirwin, Kas., on appeal from a decision of the commissioners of the general land office, in which he reverses the rulings of the department during the last administration relating to the amount of timber allowable on timber culture land entries. Hair in his application makes On the land in question, there are two Cottonwood sprouts two inches in diameter. The section was returned by the official surveyor as timber land under the ruling of the department that "was devoid of timber" in the timber culture act necessarily means "without timber" or "destitute of timber." The entry was therefore not allowed. The secretary says: "An arbitrary rule can not be established for the government of every case. It should be the desire of the department to ascertain what the intent and purpose of congress was in the passage of an act, and it stands to reason that it was not the purpose to deprive occupants of the vast prairies of the west of the counties of the act it there happens to be a single tree upon the section. I take it that the words "prairie land or land devoid of timber" within the spirit of the act means lands principally here, and to give the act the construction placed upon it by the last decision just cited, according to its legitimate deductions, a fair conclusion would prevent the entry of any prairie land that had timber of any character upon it, standing, fallen or otherwise. This certainly is too narrow a view to take of the act." As the testimony in this case is conflicting as to the amount of timber on the claim, further examination is ordered.
